title: Filtered-CoPhy: Unsupervised Learning of Counterfactual Physics in Pixel Space

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## **FILTERED-COPHY** : UNSUPERVISED LEARNING OF COUNTERFACTUAL PHYSICS IN PIXEL SPACE

## **Steeven Janny**

LIRIS, INSA Lyon, France steeven.janny@insa-lyon.fr

**Fabien Baradel Natalia Neverova** Naver Labs Europe, France Meta AI fabien.baradel@naverlabs.com nneverova@fb.com

**Madiha Nadri** LAGEPP, Univ. Lyon 1, France madiha.nadri-wolf@univ-lyon1.fr

**Greg Mori Christian Wolf** Simon Fraser Univ., Canada LIRIS, INSA Lyon, France mori@cs.sfu.ca christian.wolf@insa-lyon.fr

## ABSTRACT

Learning causal relationships in high-dimensional data (images, videos) is a hard task, as they are often defined on low-dimensional manifolds and must be extracted from complex signals dominated by appearance, lighting, textures and also spurious correlations in the data. We present a method for learning counterfactual reasoning of physical processes in pixel space, which requires the prediction of the impact of interventions on initial conditions. Going beyond the identification of structural relationships, we deal with the challenging problem of forecasting raw video over long horizons. Our method does not require the knowledge or supervision of any ground truth positions or other object or scene properties. Our model learns and acts on a suitable hybrid latent representation based on a combination of dense features, sets of 2D keypoints and an additional latent vector per keypoint. We show that this better captures the dynamics of physical processes than purely dense or sparse representations. We introduce a new challenging and carefully designed counterfactual benchmark for predictions in pixel space and outperform strong baselines in physics-inspired ML and video prediction.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Reasoning on complex, multi-modal and high-dimensional data is a natural ability of humans and other intelligent agents (Martin-Ordas et al., 2008), and one of the most important and difficult challenges of AI. While machine learning is well suited for capturing regularities in high-dimensional signals, in particular by using high-capacity deep networks, some applications also require an accurate modeling of causal relationships. This is particularly relevant in physics, where causation is considered as a fundamental axiom. In the context of machine learning, correctly capturing or modeling causal relationships can also lead to more robust predictions, in particular better generalization to out-of-distribution samples, indicating that a model has overcome the exploitation of biases and shortcuts in the training data. In recent literature on physics-inspired machine learning, causality has often been forced through the addition of prior knowledge about the physical laws that govern the studied phenomena, e.g. (Yin et al., 2021). A similar idea lies behind structured causal models, widely used in the causal inference community, where domain experts model these relationships directly in a graphical notation. This particular line of work allows to perform predictions beyond statistical forecasting, for instance by predicting unobserved counterfactuals, the impact of unobserved interventions (Balke & Pearl, 1994) — “ _What alternative outcome would have happened, if the observed event X had been replaced with an event Y (after an intervention)_ ”. Counterfactuals are interesting, as causality intervenes through the effective modification of an outcome. As an example, taken from (Sch¨olkopf et al., 2021), an agent can identify the direction of a causal relationship between an umbrella and rain from the fact that removing an umbrella will not affect the weather.

We focus on counterfactual reasoning on high-dimensional signals, in particular videos of complex physical processes. Learning such causal interactions from data is a challenging task, as spurious correlations are naturally and easily picked up by trained models. Previous work in this direction

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

was restricted to discrete outcomes, as in _CLEVRER_ (Yi et al., 2020), or to the prediction of 3D trajectories, as in _CoPhy_ (Baradel et al., 2020), which also requires supervision of object positions. In this work, we address the hard problem of predicting the alternative (counterfactual) outcomes of physical processes in pixel space, i.e. we forecast sequences of 2D projective views of the 3D scene, requiring the prediction over long horizons (150 frames corresponding to _∼_ 6 seconds). We conjecture that causal relationships can be modeled on a low dimensional manifold of the data, and propose a suitable latent representation for the causal model, in particular for the estimation of the confounders and the dynamic model itself. Similar to V-CDN (Kulkarni et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020), our latent representation is based on the unsupervised discovery of keypoints, complemented by additional information in our case. Indeed, while keypoint-based representations can easily be encoded from visual input, as stable mappings from images to points arise naturally, we claim that they are not the most suitable representation for dynamic models. We identified and addressed two principal problems: (i) the individual points of a given set are discriminated through their 2D positions only, therefore shape, geometry and relationships between multiple moving objects need to be encoded through the relative positions of points to each other, and (ii) the optimal representation for a physical dynamic model is not necessarily a 2D keypoint space, where the underlying object dynamics has also been subject to the imaging process (projective geometry).

We propose a new counterfactual model, which learns a sparse representation of visual input in the form of 2D keypoints coupled with a (small) set of coefficients per point modeling complementary shape and appearance information. Confounders (object masses and initial velocities) in the studied problem are extracted from this representation, and a learned dynamic model forecasts the entire trajectory of these keypoints from a single (counterfactual) observation. Building on recent work in data-driven analysis of dynamic systems (Janny et al., 2021; Peralez & Nadri, 2021), the dynamic model is presented in a higher-dimensional state space, where dynamics are less complex. We show, that these design choices are key to the performance of our model, and that they significantly improve the capability to perform long-term predictions. Our proposed model outperforms strong baselines for physics-informed learning of video prediction.

We introduce a new challenging dataset for this problem, which builds on _CoPhy_ , a recent counterfactual physics benchmark (Baradel et al., 2020). We go beyond the prediction of sequences of 3D positions and propose a counterfactual task for predictions in pixel space after interventions on initial conditions (displacing, re-orienting or removing objects). In contrast to the literature, our benchmark also better controls for the identifiability of causal relationships and counterfactual variables and provides more accurate physics simulation.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Counterfactual (CF) reasoning** — and learning of causal relationships in ML was made popular by works of J. Pearl, e.g. (Pearl, 2000), which motivate and introduce mathematical tools detailing the principles of _do-calculus_ , i.e. study of unobserved interventions on data. A more recent survey links these concepts to the literature in ML (Sch¨olkopf et al., 2021). The last years have seen the emergence of several benchmarks for CF reasoning in physics. CLEVRER (Yi et al., 2020) is a visual question answering dataset, where an agent is required to answer a CF question after observing a video showing 3D objects moving and colliding. Li et al. (2020) introduce a CF benchmark with two tasks: a scenario where balls interact with each other according to unknown interaction laws (such as gravity or elasticity), and a scenario where clothes are folded by the wind. The agent needs to identify CF variables and causal relationships between objects, and to predict future frames. CoPhy (Baradel et al., 2020) clearly dissociates the observed experiment from the CF one, and contains three complex 3D scenarios involving rigid body dynamics. However, the proposed method relies on the supervision of 3D object positions, while our work does not require any meta data.

**Physics-inspired ML** — and learning visual dynamics has been dealt early on with recurrent models (Srivastava et al., 2015; Finn et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2017), or GANs (Vondrick et al., 2016; Mathieu et al., 2016). Kwon & Park (2019) adopt a Cycle-GAN with two discriminator heads, in charge of identifying false images and false sequences in order to improve the temporal consistency of the model in long term prediction. Nonetheless, the integration of causal reasoning and prior knowledge in these models is not straightforward. Typical work in physics-informed models relies on disentanglement between physics-informed features and residual features (Villegas et al., 2017a;

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Denton & Birodkar, 2017) and may incorporate additional information based on the available priors on the scene (Villegas et al., 2017b; Walker et al., 2017). PhyDNet Le Guen & Thome (2020) explicitly disentangles visual features from dynamical features, which are supposed to follow a PDE. It achieves SOTA performance on Human3.6M (Ionescu et al., 2014) and Sea Surface Temperature (de Bezenac et al., 2018), but we show that it fails on our challenging benchmark.

**Keypoint detection** — is a well researched problem in vision with widely used handcrafted baselines (Lowe, 1999). New unsupervised variants emerged recently and have been shown to provide a suitable object-centric representation, close to attention models, which simplify the use of physical and/or geometric priors (Locatello et al., 2020; Veerapaneni et al., 2020). They are of interest in robotics and reinforcement learning, where a physical agent has to interact with objects (Kulkarni et al., 2019; Manuelli et al., 2020; 2019). KeypointsNet (Suwajanakorn et al., 2018) is a geometric reasoning framework, which discovers meaningful keypoints in 3D through spatial coherence between viewpoints. Close to our work, (Minderer et al., 2019) proposes to learn a keypoints-based stochastic dynamic model. However, the model is not suited for CF reasoning in physics and may suffer from inconsistency in the prediction of dynamics over long horizons.

## 3 THE **FILTERED-COPHY** BENCHMARK

We build on _CoPhy_ (Baradel et al., 2020), retaining its strengths, but explicitly focusing on a counterfactual scenario in pixel space and eliminating the ill-posedness of tasks we identified in the existing work. Each data sample is called an _experiment_ , represented as a pair of trajectories: an _observed_ one with initial condition _X_ 0 = **A** and outcome _Xt_ =1 _..T_ = **B** (a sequence), and a _counterfactual_ one _X_[¯] 0 = **C** and _X_[¯] _t_ =1 _..T_ = **D** (a sequence). Throughout this paper we will use the letters **A** _,_ **B** _,_ **C** and **D** to distinguish the different parts of each experiment. The initial conditions **A** and **C** are linked through a _do-operator do_ ( _X_ 0 = **C** ), which modifies the initial condition (Pearl, 2018). Experiments are parameterized by a set of intrinsic physical parameters _z_ which are not observable from a single initial image **A** . We refer to these as _confounders_ . As in _CoPhy_ , in our benchmark the do-operator is observed during training, but confounders are not — they have been used to generate the data, but are not used during training or testing. Following (Pearl, 2018), the counterfactual task consists in inferring the counterfactual outcome **D** given the observed trajectory **AB** and the counterfactual initial state **C** , following a three-step process:
-  **Abduction** : use the observed data **AB** to compute the counterfactual variables, i.e. physical parameters, which are not affected by the do-operation.
-  **Action** : update the causal model; keep the same identified confounders and apply the dooperator, i.e. replace the initial state **A** by **C** .

-  **Prediction** : Compute the counterfactual outcome **D** using the causal graph.

The benchmark contains three scenarios involving rigid body dynamics. BlocktowerCF studies stable and unstable 3D cube towers, the confounders are masses. BallsCF focuses on 2D collisions between moving spheres (confounders are masses and initial velocities). CollisionCF is about collisions between a sphere and a cylinder (confounders are masses and initial velocities) (Fig. 1).

Unlike _CoPhy_ , our benchmark involves predictions in RGB pixel space only. The do-operation consists in visually observable interventions on **A** , such as moving or removing an object. The confounders cannot be identified from the single-frame observation **A** , identification requires the analysis of the entire **AB** trajectory.

**Identifiability of confounders** — For an experiment ( **AB** _,_ **CD** _, z_ ) to be well-posed, the confounders _z_ must be retrievable from **AB** . For example, since the masses of a stable cube tower cannot be identified generally in all situations, it can be impossible to predict the counterfactual outcome of an unstable tower, as collisions are not resolvable without known masses. In contrast to _CoPhy_ , we ensure that each experiment _ψ_ : ( _X_ 0 _, z_ ) _�→ Xt_ =1 _..T_ , given initial condition _X_ 0 and confounders _z_ , is well posed and satisfies the following constraints:

In other words, we impose the existence of an object of the scene for which the (unobserved) physical properties have a determining effect on the trajectory. Details on how this constraint was enforced are given in appendix A.2.

**Temporal resolution** — the physical laws we target involve highly non-linear phenomena, in particular collision and resting contacts. Collisions are difficult to learn because their actions are both intense, brief, and highly non-linear, depending on the geometry of the objects in 3D space. The temporal resolution of physical simulations is of prime importance. A parallel can be made with Nyquist-Shannon frequency, as a

trajectory sampled with too low frequency cannot be reconstructed with precision. We simulate and record trajectories at 25 FPS, compared to 5 FPS chosen in _CoPhy_ , justified with two experiments. Firstly, Fig. 2 shows the trajectories of the center of masses of cubes in BlocktowerCF, colored dots are shown at 25 FPS and black dots at 5 FPS. We can see that collisions with the ground fall below the sampling rate of 5 FPS, making it hard to infer physical laws from regularities in data at this frequency. A second experiment involves learning a prediction model at different frequencies, confirming the choice 25 FPS — details are given in appendix A.3.

## 4 UNSUPERVISED LEARNING OF COUNTERFACTUAL PHYSICS

We introduce a new model for counterfactual learning of physical processes capable of predicting visual sequences **D** in the image space over long horizons. The method does not require any supervision other than videos of observed and counterfactual experiences. The code is publicly available online at **https://filteredcophy.github.io** . The model consists of three parts, learning the latent representation and its (counterfactual) dynamics:
- **The encoder (De-Rendering module)** learns a hybrid representation of an image in the form of a (i) dense feature map and (ii) 2D keypoints combined with (iii) a low-dimensional vector of coef-

## 4.1 DISENTANGLING VISUAL INFORMATION FROM DYNAMICS

The encoder takes an input image **X** and predicts a representation with three streams, sharing a common conv. backbone, as shown in Fig. 3. We propose an unsupervised training objective, which favors the emergence of a latent representation disentangling static and dynamic information.
1. A dense feature map **F** = _F_ ( **X** ), which contains static information, such as the background.
2. A set of 2D keypoints **k** = _K_ ( **X** ), which carry positional information from moving objects.
3. A set of corresponding coefficients **c** = _C_ ( **X** ), one vector **c** _k_ of size _C_ per keypoint _k_ , which encodes orientation and appearance information.

The unsupervised objective is formulated on pairs of images ( **X** source _,_ **X** target) randomly sampled from same **D** sequences (see appendix D.1 for details on sampling). Exploiting an assumption on the absence of camera motion[1] , the goal is to favor the emergence of disentangling static and dynamic information. To this end, both images are encoded, and the reconstruction of the target image is predicted with a decoder _D_ fusing the source dense feature map and the target keypoints and coefficients. This formulation requires the decoder to aggregate dense information from the source and sparse values from the target, naturally leading to motion being predicted by the latter.

On the decoder _D_ side, we add inductive bias, which favors the usage of the 2D keypoint information in a spatial way. The 2D coordinates **k** _k_ for each keypoint _k_ are encoded as Gaussian heatmaps _G_ ( **k** _k_ ), i.e. 2D Gaussian functions centered on the keypoint position. **Related work** – our unsupervised objective is somewhat related to _Transporter_ (Kulkarni et al., 2019), which, as our model, computes visual feature vectors _F_ source and _F_ target as well as 2D keypoints _K_ source and _K_ target, modeled as a 2D vector via Gaussian mapping. It leverages a handcrafted transport equation: Ψ[ˆ] target= _F_ source _×_ (1 _−K_ source) _×_ (1 _−K_ target) + _F_ target _×K_ target. As in our case, the target image is reconstructed through a refiner network _X_[ˆ] target = _R_ (Ψ[ˆ] target). The transporter suffers from a major drawback when used for video prediction, as it requires parts of the target image to reconstruct the target image — the model was originally proposed in the context of RL and control, where reconstruction is not an objective. It also does not use shape coefficients, requiring shapes to be encoded by several keypoints, or abusively be carried through the dense features _F_ target. This typically leads to complex dynamics non representative of the dynamical objects. We conducted an in-depth comparison between the Transporter and our representation in appendix C.2.

## 4.2 DYNAMIC MODEL AND CONFOUNDER ESTIMATION

Our counterfactual dynamic model (CoDy) leverages multiple graph network (GN) based modules (Battaglia et al., 2016) that join forces to solve the counterfactual forecasting tasks of **FilteredCoPhy** . Each one of these networks is a classical GN, abbreviated as _GN_ ( **x** _k_ ), which contextualizes input node embeddings **x** _k_ through incoming edge interactions **e** _ik_ , providing output node embeddings **ˆx** _k_ (parameters are not shared over the instances):

In the lines of (Baradel et al., 2020), given the original initial condition and outcome **AB** , CoDy estimates an unsupervised representation **u** _k_ of the latent confounder variables per keypoint _k_ through the counterfactual estimator ( _CF estimator_ in Fig. 4). We then model the temporal evolution of this representation with a gated recurrent unit (Cho et al., 2014) per keypoint, sharing parameters over keypoints, taking as input the sequence **h** _k_ . Its last hidden vector is taken as the confounder estimate **u** _k_ .

Recent works on the Koopman Operator (Lusch et al., 2018) and Kazantzis-Kravaris-Luenberger Observer (Janny et al., 2021; Peralez & Nadri, 2021) have theoretically shown that, under mild assumptions, there exists a latent space of higher dimension, where a dynamical system given as an EDP can have a simpler dynamics. Inspired by this idea, we used an encoder-decoder structure within CoDy, which projects our dynamic system into a higher-dimensional state space, performs forecasting of the dynamics in this latent space, and then projects predictions back to the original keypoint space. Note that this dynamics encoder/decoder is different from the encoder/decoder of the de-rendering/rendering modules discussed in section 4.1. The state encoder _E_ ( **s** ( _t_ )) = _**σ**_ ( _t_ ) is modeled as a graph network _GN_ , whose aggregation function projects into an output embedding space _**σ**_ ( _t_ ) of dimension 256. The decoder ∆( _**σ**_ ( _t_ )) = **s** ( _t_ ) temporally processes the individual contextualized states _**σ**_ ( _t_ ) with a GRU, followed by new contextualization with a graph network _GN_ . Details on the full architecture are provided in appendix D.2.

The dynamic model CoDy performs forecasting in the higher-dimensional space _**σ**_ ( _t_ ), computing a displacement vector _**δ**_ ( _t_ + 1) such that _**σ**_ ( _t_ + 1) = _**σ**_ ( _t_ ) + _**δ**_ ( _t_ + 1). ## 4.3 TRAINING

End-to-end training of all three modules jointly is challenging, as the same pipeline controls both the keypoint-based state representation and the dynamic module (CoDy), involving two adversarial objectives: optimizing reconstruction pushes the keypoint encoding to be as representative as possible, but learning the dynamics favors a simple representation. Faced with these two contradictory tasks, the model is numerically unstable and rapidly converges to regression to the mean. As described above, we separately train the encoder+decoder pair without dynamic information on reconstruction only, c.f. Equation (4). Then we freeze the parameters of the keypoint detector and train CoDy to forecast the keypoints from **D** minimizing the loss in Equation (6).

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We compare the proposed model to three strong baselines for physics-inspired video prediction.

- **PhyDNet** (Le Guen & Thome, 2020) is a non-counterfactual video prediction model that forecasts future frames using a decomposition between (a) a feature vector that temporally evolves via an LSTM and (b) a dynamic state that follows a PDE learned through specifically designed cells.

Figure 5: Network _R_ learns Table 3: Impact of having additional orientation/shape coa distortion from multiple oriefficients ( ) compared to the keypoint-only solution ( ), ented ellipses to target shapes. for different numbers of keypoints: equal to number of objects (= _N_ ), 2 _N_ and 4 _N_ .

- **V-CDN** (Li et al., 2020) is a counterfactual model based on keypoints, close to our work. It identifies confounders from the beginning of a sequence and learns a keypoint predictor through auto-encoding using the Transporter equation (see discussion in Sect. 4.1). As it is, it cannot be used for video prediction and is incomparable with our work, see details in appendix C. We therefore replace the Transporter by our own de-rendering/rendering modules, from which we remove the additional coefficients. We refer to this model as UV-CDN (for Unsupervised V-CDN).
- **PredRNN** (Wang et al., 2017) is a ConvLSTM-based video prediction model that leverages spatial and temporal memories a through a spatiotemporal LSTM cell.

All models have been implemented in PyTorch, architectures are described in appendix D. For the baselines PhyDNet, UV-CDN and PredRNN, we used the official source code provided by the authors. We evaluate on each scenario of **Filtered-CoPhy** on the counterfactual video prediction task. For the two counterfactual models (Ours and UV-CDN), we evaluate on the tasks as intended: we provide the observed sequence **AB** and the CF initial condition **C** , and forecast the sequence **D** . The non-CF baselines are required to predict the entire video from a single frame, in order to prevent them from leveraging shortcuts in a part of the video and bypass the need for physical reasoning.

We measure performance with time-averaged peak signal-to noise ratio ( **PSNR** ) that directly measures reconstruction quality. However, this metric is mainly dominated by error on the static background, which is not our main interest. We also introduce Localized PSNR ( **L-PSNR** ), which measures area error on the important regions near moving objects, computed on masked images. We compute the masks using classical background subtraction techniques.

**Comparison to the SOTA** — We compare our model against UV-CDN, PhyDNet and PredRNN in Table 1, consistently and significantly outperforming the baselines. The gap with UV-CDN is particularly interesting, as it confirms the choice of additional coefficients to model the dynamics of moving objects. PredRNN shows competitive performances, especially on collisionCF. However, our localized PSNR tends to indicates that the baseline does not reconstruct accurately the foreground, favoring the reconstruction of the background to the detriment of the dynamics of the scene. Fig. 6 visualizes the prediction on a single example, more can be found in appendix G. We also compare to trivial copying baselines in Table 2, namely Copy B, which assumes no intervention and outputs the **B** sequence, and Copy C, which assumes a stable tower. We evaluate these models in keypoints space measuring MSE on keypoints and coefficients averaged over time, as copying baselines are unbeatable in the regions of static background, making the PSNR metrics unusable.

We provide additional empirical results by comparing the models using Multi-object Tracking metrics and studies on the impact of the do-operations on PSNR in appendix E. We also compute an upper bound of our model using CoPhyNet baseline as described in Baradel et al. (2020).
**Performance on real-world data** — is reported in appendix F, showing experiments on 516 videos of real wooden blocks introduced in (Lerer et al., 2016).

**Impact of appearance coefficients** — are reported in Table 3, comparing to the baseline using a keypoint-only representation. The coefficients have a significant impact: even increasing the number of keypoints to compensate for the loss of information cannot overcome the advantage of disentangling positions and shapes, as done in our model. **Learning filters** — does not have a positive impact on reconstruction performance compared to the choice of the handcrafted bank, as can be seen in table 5. We conjecture that the additional degrees of freedom are redundant with parts of the filter kernels in the refinement module _R_ : this corresponds to jointly learning a multi-channel representation _{G[i] k[}][i] k_[=1] =1 _[..C] ..K_[for][shapes][as][well][as][the][mapping] which geometrically distorts them into the target object shapes. Fixing the latent representation does not constrain the system, as the mapping _R_ can adjust to it — see Fig. 5.

**Impact of the high-dimensional dynamic space** — We evaluate the impact of modeling object dynamics in high-dimensional space through the CoDy encoder in Table 4, comparing projection to 256 dimensions to the baseline reasoning directly in keypoint + coefficient space. The experiment confirms this choice of KKL-like encoder (Janny et al., 2021).

## 6 CONCLUSION

We introduced a new benchmark for counterfactual reasoning in physical processes requiring to perform video prediction, i.e. predicting raw pixel observations over a long horizon. The benchmark has been carefully designed and generated imposing constraints on identifiability and counterfactuality. We also propose a new method for counterfactual reasoning, which is based on a hybrid latent representation combining 2D keypoints and additional latent vectors encoding appearance and shape. We introduce an unsupervised learning algorithm for this representation, which does not require any supervision on confounders or other object properties and processes raw video. Counterfactual prediction of video frames remains a challenging task, and Filtered CoPhy still exhibits failures in maintaining rigid structures of objects over long prediction time-scales. We hope that our benchmark will inspire further breakthroughs in this domain.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel and ambitious approach to unsupervised counterfactual reasoning in pixel space, extending prior work like CoPhy with a hybrid latent representation and a carefully designed benchmark. Its strength lies in the integration of disentangled visual and dynamic modeling, and the clear motivation for improving temporal resolution and identifiability. However, the evaluation does not fully validate the core claims about counterfactual accuracy or disentanglement, leaving key assumptions untested.

Overview issues:

- Lack of disentanglement evaluation despite central claim: The paper builds on the premise that its hybrid latent space disentangles visual appearance from physical dynamics, yet provides no quantitative or qualitative evidence for this. Equations 3 and 4 describe a coefficient-based deformation of keypoint heatmaps intended to encode shape and orientation, but no ablation or visualization shows that these coefficients actually capture appearance independently of motion. Without standard disentanglement metrics (e.g., DCI, MIG) or qualitative manipulations of coefficients while fixing keypoints, the claim remains unsupported. This is a central weakness, as the entire model architecture rests on this separation. The authors should include a controlled experiment showing that modifying coefficients changes appearance without affecting dynamics, and vice versa.

- Counterfactual accuracy not directly measured: The evaluation relies heavily on PSNR and L-PSNR, which assess reconstruction quality but not counterfactual correctness. A model could reproduce plausible-looking videos while failing to capture the correct physics under intervention. For instance, in Table 2, the Copy C baseline performs poorly in MSE on keypoints, but the paper does not analyze whether the model’s predictions reflect the true causal effect of object removal or displacement. There is no comparison to ground-truth counterfactual trajectories in terms of physical parameters (e.g., mass, velocity) or event timing (e.g., collapse onset). To validate counterfactual reasoning, the authors should report metrics on predicted confounders or event timing, not just pixel-level fidelity.

- Unjustified assumption of confounder identifiability from video: The paper assumes that confounders like mass and initial velocity can be accurately inferred from the AB trajectory alone (Abduction step), but provides no evidence that this is feasible in practice. While Definition 1 gives a theoretical identifiability condition, real-world estimation from pixel data may be ill-posed due to noise, occlusion, or symmetries in dynamics. The model’s performance on Copy B and Copy C baselines suggests it goes beyond trivial copying, but this does not prove accurate confounder recovery. A failure mode—such as misestimating mass in BlocktowerCF leading to incorrect stability prediction—is not analyzed. The authors should include a diagnostic experiment, such as regressing estimated latent confounders against ground-truth values in simulation.

- Dynamic model evaluation conflates reconstruction and prediction: The dynamic model is trained and evaluated on predicting keypoint + coefficient trajectories from the ground-truth encoder (Equation 6), but in practice, it operates on noisy, learned representations. This creates a mismatch: the model may succeed by fitting clean trajectories while failing when chained with the encoder. The end-to-end performance (pixel prediction) is evaluated, but there is no intermediate evaluation of the CoDy model’s accuracy when fed encoded representations from the de-rendering module. This obscures whether errors stem from representation learning or dynamics modeling. The authors should report keypoint prediction error when the full pipeline is used, not just the dynamics model on ground-truth states.

- Filter bank design choice lacks empirical justification: The model uses a fixed filter bank H based on handcrafted kernels (horizontal, vertical, diagonal), arguing that learning it harms performance (Table 5). However, the reported PSNRs in that table are worse for the fixed bank, contradicting the claim. Table 5 shows 'Fixed' filter bank achieving lower PSNR than 'Learned' in all cases, yet the text interprets this as support for fixed filters. This is inconsistent. Either the table is mislabeled or the interpretation is flawed. The authors must correct this discrepancy and clarify whether learned filters hurt or help reconstruction.

- No quantitative disentanglement validation: The paper claims its hybrid latent representation disentangles visual appearance from physical dynamics, but provides no quantitative or qualitative evidence that coefficients encode appearance independently of motion. This is central to the model's design, yet standard disentanglement metrics like DCI or MIG are absent. Readers will question whether the coefficients truly capture shape and orientation rather than just co-varying with position. The authors should compute MIG or DCI scores on a held-out set of interventions, or present a visualization showing that modifying coefficients while holding keypoints fixed alters object appearance but not motion in the rendered output.

- Counterfactual reasoning not directly evaluated: Performance is measured via PSNR and L-PSNR, which assess pixel-level fidelity but not causal correctness. A model could generate visually plausible videos that misrepresent the physical outcome of an intervention—such as failing to predict a collapse in BlocktowerCF when mass confounders are misestimated. The paper needs a metric tied to causal validity, such as accuracy in predicting event timing (e.g., first contact, collapse) or success in recovering ground-truth counterfactual trajectories in simulation. The authors should report event prediction accuracy or physical parameter recovery error on synthetic data where ground truth is available.

- Confounder estimation not validated against ground truth: The model assumes it can infer latent confounders (mass, initial velocity) from observed trajectories, but there is no evidence that these are accurately recovered. Without linking estimated latents to true values, the claim of unsupervised confounder discovery remains unsubstantiated. In simulation, the authors have access to ground-truth confounders and could regress their estimated latent vectors against them. They should plot correlation between learned representations and true mass/velocity across a test set, or show that ablation of coefficients degrades confounder recovery in a controlled setting.

- Dynamic model evaluated on clean states, not learned representations: The CoDy model is trained and tested on ground-truth keypoint and coefficient sequences, but in practice, it depends on noisy, learned encodings from the de-rendering module. This creates a mismatch between training and deployment conditions. The paper should report keypoint prediction error when the full pipeline is run end-to-end, including the encoder, to assess robustness to representation noise. Ablating the state auto-encoder (Table 4) helps, but does not close the loop—evaluation must include the full learned representation to reflect real usage.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide quantitative or qualitative evidence that the coefficient vectors in the latent space disentangle appearance and shape from dynamics, using metrics like MIG or controlled latent traversals.

- Add evaluation of counterfactual accuracy beyond pixel reconstruction, such as correctness of predicted physical events (e.g., collapse timing) or fidelity of estimated confounders against ground truth.

- Include an analysis of confounder estimation quality, showing whether latent variables correlate with true physical parameters like mass or velocity in simulation.

- Correct the inconsistency in Table 5 and clarify the impact of learned vs. fixed filter banks on reconstruction performance.

- Report keypoint prediction error in the CoDy model when using encoded representations from the de-rendering module, not just ground-truth states.

comments.title: Misleading identifiability condition in Definition 1
comments.quote:
Definition 1

(Identifiability, *(Pearl, 2018)*) The experiment $(\mathbf{AB},\mathbf{CD},z)$ is identifiable if, for any set of confounders $z^{\prime}$:

$\psi(\mathbf{A},z)=\psi(\mathbf{A},z^{\prime})\Rightarrow\psi(\mathbf{C},z)=\psi(\mathbf{C},z^{\prime}).$ (1)
comments.explanation: The stated condition does not ensure identifiability in the standard sense. True identifiability requires that the confounders $z$ be uniquely determined by the observed trajectory $\mathbf{AB}$, i.e., $\psi(\mathbf{A},z) = \psi(\mathbf{A},z') \Rightarrow z = z'$. The current formulation only ensures counterfactual consistency: if two confounder values produce the same observed outcome, they must produce the same counterfactual outcome. But this allows multiple distinct $z$ to explain $\mathbf{AB}$ equally well, meaning $z$ is not actually identifiable. This weakens the theoretical foundation of the benchmark. The definition should either be corrected to reflect actual identifiability or renamed to 'counterfactual consistency' with a separate discussion of identifiability assumptions.

comments.title: Counterfactuality definition misaligned with causal intent
comments.quote:
Definition 2 (Counterfactuality). Let  $z^k$  be the set of confounders  $z$ , where the  $k^{th}$  value has been modified. The experiment (AB, CD,  $z$ ) is counterfactual if and only if:

$$\exists k: \psi (\mathbf{C}, z^k) \neq \psi (\mathbf{C}, z). \tag{2}$$
comments.explanation: This definition checks whether perturbing a confounder changes the outcome under intervention $\mathbf{C}$, but that measures sensitivity, not counterfactuality. A true counterfactual experiment should compare outcomes under different initial conditions $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{C}$ with the same $z$. The correct condition is $\psi(\mathbf{C}, z) \neq \psi(\mathbf{A}, z)$, which ensures the intervention leads to a different physical outcome. As written, the definition could classify non-interventional cases (e.g., $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A}$) as counterfactual if $z$ is sensitive, which defeats the purpose. The current form conflates parameter sensitivity with causal effect and should be revised to reflect actual counterfactual divergence.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about CoDy training input
comments.quote: Then we freeze the parameters of the keypoint detector and train CoDy to forecast the keypoints from  $\mathbf{D}$  minimizing the loss in Equation (6).
comments.explanation: This statement misrepresents the training procedure. $\mathbf{D}$ is the counterfactual outcome sequence—the target to be predicted—not an input to the model. Training CoDy on $\mathbf{D}$ would imply inverse or reverse-time dynamics, contradicting the forward prediction setup. The correct input should be the encoded representation of the observed $\mathbf{AB}$ sequence, from which the model forecasts $\mathbf{CD}$. This error undermines clarity about the causal pipeline. The sentence must be corrected to reflect that CoDy is trained to predict future states from $\mathbf{s^{AB}}(t)$, not from $\mathbf{D}$, to maintain consistency with the counterfactual reasoning framework.

comments.title: Unjustified interpretation of filter bank results
comments.quote: Learning filters — does not have a positive impact on reconstruction performance compared to the choice of the handcrafted bank, as can be seen in table 5. We conjecture that the additional degrees of freedom are redundant with parts of the filter kernels in the refinement module  $\mathcal{R}$ : this corresponds to jointly learning a multi-channel representation  $\{G_k^i\}_{k=1..K}^{i=1..C}$  for shapes as well as the mapping which geometrically distorts them into the target object shapes.
comments.explanation: While Table 5 shows that learned filters underperform fixed ones, the explanation attributing this to redundancy in $\mathcal{R}$ is speculative and unsupported. The data only show a performance gap, not its cause—optimization instability, overfitting, or poor initialization could equally explain the result. The authors conflate empirical observation with mechanistic insight without ablation or analysis. The interpretation should be revised to acknowledge that learned filters degrade performance, but the reason remains unclear unless further evidence is provided. Presenting conjecture as explanation risks misleading readers about model behavior.

comments.title: Flawed comparison to UV-CDN due to architectural hybridization
comments.quote: We therefore replace the Transporter by our own de-rendering/rendering modules, from which we remove the additional coefficients. We refer to this model as UV-CDN (for Unsupervised V-CDN).
comments.explanation: The UV-CDN baseline is not a faithful adaptation of V-CDN but a hybrid model that uses the proposed de-rendering and rendering architecture while removing the key innovation (appearance coefficients). This creates an unfair comparison: the performance gap may stem from superior rendering or dynamic modeling in the proposed framework, not the coefficients themselves. A valid ablation would keep all components identical and only vary the presence of coefficients. Otherwise, the claimed advantage of the hybrid representation is confounded by architectural differences. The baseline should be redefined as a proper ablation within the same framework, or the comparison should be reframed to reflect that UV-CDN is a reimplementation, not a direct baseline.

comments.title: Ambiguous role of coefficients in disentanglement claim
comments.quote: Without any state supervision we show that the model learns a representation which encodes positions in keypoints and appearance and orientation in the coefficients.
comments.explanation: The claim that coefficients encode 'appearance and orientation' is asserted without evidence. Appearance typically includes color, texture, and material, which are unlikely to be captured by low-dimensional vectors deforming keypoint heatmaps. The dense feature map is more plausible as the carrier of appearance. Moreover, no ablation or manipulation experiment shows that modifying coefficients changes appearance without affecting dynamics. The architecture does not enforce such disentanglement. Either provide evidence—e.g., visualizations of coefficient edits—or rephrase to reflect that coefficients parameterize geometric deformations, not general appearance, to avoid overstating representational capabilities.

comments.title: Misleading claim about emergent tracking
comments.quote: Without any supervision, a tracking strategy emerges naturally through the unsupervised objective: we optimize reconstruction of  $\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{target}}$ , given features from  $\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{source}}$  and keypoints+coefficients from  $\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{target}}$ .
comments.explanation: The model does not perform tracking in any meaningful sense—it is given target keypoints directly, so no temporal correspondence needs to be learned. True unsupervised tracking would require predicting keypoint trajectories from source input alone. Instead, this is a conditional reconstruction task where motion is provided in the input. The term 'tracking strategy emerges' is therefore misleading. The objective induces appearance transfer conditioned on known spatial configurations, not motion modeling. The claim should be revised to clarify that the model leverages target keypoints during training and does not learn to track motion end-to-end.

comments.title: Nyquist-Shannon analogy misapplied to collision dynamics
comments.quote:
A parallel can be made with Nyquist-Shannon frequency, as a

trajectory sampled with too low frequency cannot be reconstructed with precision.
comments.explanation: The Nyquist-Shannon theorem applies only to band-limited signals, but collision events involve impulsive forces and discontinuous velocity changes—non-band-limited phenomena. No finite sampling rate can fully capture such events, making the analogy invalid. While higher temporal resolution (25 FPS) is empirically justified by Fig. 2, invoking Nyquist-Shannon provides false theoretical grounding. The argument should be reframed to emphasize empirical benefits—reduced aliasing of transient dynamics—without appealing to a misapplied sampling theorem.

comments.title: Incomplete definition of hybrid latent space
comments.quote:
The encoder (De-Rendering module) learns a hybrid representation of an image in the form of a (i) dense feature map and (ii) 2D keypoints combined with (iii) a low-dimensional vector of coef

![img-4.jpeg](img-4.jpeg)
Figure 3: We de-render visual input into a latent space composed of a dense feature map  $\mathbf{F}$  modeling static information, a set of keypoints  $\mathbf{k}$ , and associated coefficients  $\mathbf{c}$ . We here show the training configuration taking as input pairs  $(\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{source}}, \mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{target}})$  of images. Without any supervision, a tracking strategy emerges naturally through the unsupervised objective: we optimize reconstruction of  $\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{target}}$ , given features from  $\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{source}}$  and keypoints+coefficients from  $\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{target}}$ .

ficients, see Fig. 3. Without any state supervision we show that the model learns a representation which encodes positions in keypoints and appearance and orientation in the coefficients.
comments.explanation: The description is fragmented and fails to clearly define the coefficient vector's scope—per-keypoint or global. The sentence is cut off mid-word ('coef...ficients'), then repeated after a figure break, creating redundancy and confusion. The claim that the model 'learns a representation which encodes positions in keypoints and appearance and orientation in the coefficients' is presented as a demonstrated result without supporting evidence in this section. The paragraph should be rewritten as a single coherent passage, completing the definition of the coefficient vector and qualifying the disentanglement claim with reference to later evidence, not stating it as an established fact here.

comments.title: L-PSNR mask computation underspecified
comments.quote: We also introduce Localized PSNR (L-PSNR), which measures area error on the important regions near moving objects, computed on masked images. We compute the masks using classical background subtraction techniques.
comments.explanation: The term 'classical background subtraction techniques' is too vague. It is unclear whether masks are derived from ground-truth videos, predicted frames, or input sequences, and which algorithm (e.g., MOG2, optical flow) is used. Since L-PSNR focuses on foreground accuracy, its validity depends on mask quality. If masks rely on ground truth, the metric is inaccessible in real deployment and may bias evaluation. Specify the exact method and data source—e.g., 'Masks were computed using MOG2 on ground-truth sequences'—to ensure transparency and reproducibility.

comments.title: Table 4 omits scale in header
comments.quote: Table 4: Impact of the dynamic CoDy encoder (✓) against the baseline operating in the keypoint + coefficient space (X). We report  $\mathrm{MSE} \times 10^{-3}$  on prediction of keypoints + coefficients (4 pts).
comments.explanation: The caption notes that MSE is scaled by $10^{-3}$, but this is not reflected in the table headers or formatting. Readers may misinterpret the values (e.g., 9.58) as raw MSE, which would be implausibly low. To prevent confusion, the column headers should explicitly include the scale, e.g., 'MSE × 10⁻³'. This is a minor but important detail for accurate interpretation of quantitative results.

comments.title: Inconsistent model naming in conclusion
comments.quote: We also propose a new method for counterfactual reasoning, which is based on a hybrid latent representation combining 2D keypoints and additional latent vectors encoding appearance and shape.
comments.explanation: The model is called 'Filtered-CoPhy' in the title and abstract, but referred to here without the 'Filtered-' prefix, obscuring the connection to the filtering mechanism that is central to the method (e.g., fixed filter bank H). This inconsistency may suggest the filtering component is optional, when the authors claim it is critical. To maintain clarity and emphasize the full contribution, the sentence should include 'named Filtered-CoPhy' to align with the rest of the paper.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Filtered-CoPhy, a method for unsupervised counterfactual physics learning in pixel space, along with a new benchmark. While the approach is innovative and technically sound in several aspects, there are significant concerns about the evaluation methodology, the claimed unsupervised nature of the learning, and the generalizability of the results.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by proposing a challenging counterfactual benchmark for pixel-space physics prediction and a novel hybrid representation combining keypoints with appearance coefficients. The technical approach is well-motivated, drawing on Koopman operator theory for dynamics modeling. However, the evaluation has serious issues that undermine confidence in the claimed improvements.

Overview issues:

- Inconsistent evaluation protocol between counterfactual and non-counterfactual baselines: The paper evaluates counterfactual models (Ours, UV-CDN) using the intended AB→C→D pipeline but requires non-counterfactual baselines (PhyDNet, PredRNN) to predict from a single frame. This creates an unfair comparison, as stated in Section 5: 'The non-CF baselines are required to predict the entire video from a single frame, in order to prevent them from leveraging shortcuts.' This protocol disadvantage is severe - predicting 150 frames from one frame is fundamentally harder than having the full AB sequence context. The performance gap may largely reflect this evaluation mismatch rather than the superiority of the counterfactual approach. A fair comparison would provide all baselines with equivalent information (either AB sequence or single frame) or report additional metrics under consistent conditions.

- Questionable 'unsupervised' claim due to training data requirements: The paper claims unsupervised learning but requires paired counterfactual trajectories (AB, CD) during training. Section 4.3 states training uses 'videos of observed and counterfactual experiences' - this is significant supervision. While no explicit labels like positions are used, having access to counterfactual outcomes provides strong implicit supervision about physical parameters. The method learns confounders by observing how outcomes change under interventions, which requires knowing both the original and counterfactual results. This differs from truly unsupervised physics learning from single trajectories. The framing should be clarified to specify that supervision comes through counterfactual pairs rather than direct labels.

- Insufficient analysis of confounder estimation quality: The paper's core claim involves estimating confounders (masses, velocities) from observed trajectories, but provides no quantitative evaluation of how well these latent variables are recovered. Section 4.2 describes the CF estimator but only reports downstream prediction metrics. Without measuring confounder estimation accuracy against ground truth (which is available in the synthetic benchmark), readers cannot assess whether the model actually learns meaningful physical parameters or merely finds an effective latent representation for prediction. This analysis is crucial given the paper's emphasis on causal reasoning and the abduction step in Pearl's framework.

- Limited evaluation of robustness to visual variations: The paper claims robustness to 'visual noise and variations in raw pixel inputs' but only evaluates on clean synthetic data. The real-world experiments (appendix F) on 516 videos are mentioned but not shown in main results. There's no systematic evaluation of performance under varying lighting conditions, textures, or occlusions - factors that typically challenge pixel-space methods. The Filtered-CoPhy benchmark appears to have controlled visual conditions, which may not reflect the method's true robustness. Given the domain-specific concern about pixel-space processing robustness, this represents a significant gap in the evaluation.

- Incomplete analysis of generalization across scenarios: While the paper shows results across three scenarios, it doesn't systematically evaluate cross-scenario generalization. The models appear to be trained and tested on each scenario separately. A key test for physics learning would be training on one or two scenarios and testing on unseen configurations or object types. The literature context notes that 'scaling object-centric learning to unconstrained real-world videos remains challenging,' but the paper doesn't address whether its learned physics transfers beyond the training distributions. This limits claims about the generality of the learned physical understanding.

- No demonstration of confounder estimation accuracy: The paper's central claim involves estimating physical confounders (masses and velocities) from observed trajectories through the abduction step, but provides no quantitative evaluation of how well these latent variables are recovered. Since Filtered-CoPhy is a synthetic benchmark with known ground truth confounders, the authors should report estimation accuracy against these true values. Without this analysis, readers cannot determine whether the model actually learns meaningful physical parameters or merely finds an effective latent representation for prediction. The paper should add experiments measuring the correlation or error between estimated confounders and ground truth for at least one scenario (e.g., BallsCF), which would directly support the causal reasoning claims.

- Missing worked example of identifiability enforcement: The paper introduces formal identifiability constraints (Definition 1) and counterfactuality requirements (Definition 2) for Filtered-CoPhy, but never demonstrates these with a concrete example. Readers would benefit from seeing a specific trajectory where different confounder values would lead to different counterfactual outcomes, and how the data generation ensures this. The paper should walk through one BlocktowerCF example showing: (1) specific mass values, (2) the observed trajectory AB, (3) a counterfactual intervention C, and (4) how different mass values would produce different outcomes D. This would make the identifiability concept concrete and justify why the benchmark avoids ill-posed cases.

- No quantitative counterfactual reasoning evaluation: While the paper reports video prediction metrics (PSNR, L-PSNR), it doesn't directly evaluate the counterfactual reasoning capability beyond reconstruction quality. A proper evaluation would test whether the model correctly predicts how specific interventions change outcomes, not just overall video fidelity. The paper should add metrics measuring counterfactual accuracy, such as: (1) whether predicted collision outcomes match ground truth under interventions, (2) error in predicting final object positions after interventions, or (3) binary accuracy on whether the tower falls/stays upright in BlocktowerCF. This would directly assess the counterfactual reasoning rather than conflating it with general video prediction performance.

- Missing implementation details for practical adoption: The paper claims practical relevance for counterfactual physics prediction but provides insufficient guidance for implementation. Key practical details are missing: (1) how to choose the number of keypoints and coefficients for new scenarios, (2) computational requirements for the 150-frame predictions, (3) sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters γ₁, γ₂, γ₃, and (4) failure modes when assumptions break down. The paper should include a section discussing these practical considerations, perhaps with ablation studies showing how performance scales with computational budget and guidance for applying the method to new domains beyond the three benchmark scenarios.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Implement consistent evaluation protocols for all baselines, providing both AB sequence and single-frame conditions to fairly assess the contribution of counterfactual reasoning

- Clarify the supervision requirements and reframe claims about 'unsupervised' learning to accurately reflect the use of counterfactual trajectory pairs

- Add quantitative evaluation of confounder estimation accuracy against ground truth parameters in the synthetic benchmark

- Include systematic robustness tests under varying visual conditions (lighting, noise, textures) and provide the real-world results in the main paper

- Conduct cross-scenario generalization experiments to demonstrate the transferability of learned physics beyond training distributions

comments.title: Inconsistent evaluation protocol between counterfactual and non-counterfactual baselines
comments.quote: For the two counterfactual models (Ours and UV-CDN), we evaluate on the tasks as intended: we provide the observed sequence AB and the CF initial condition C, and forecast the sequence D. The non-CF baselines are required to predict the entire video from a single frame, in order to prevent them from leveraging shortcuts in a part of the video and bypass the need for physical reasoning.
comments.explanation: The evaluation protocol creates an unfair comparison between counterfactual and non-counterfactual models. Counterfactual models receive the full AB sequence plus initial condition C as input, while non-CF baselines must predict 150 frames from only a single frame. This fundamental input disparity means the performance gap may reflect the evaluation design rather than model superiority. The paper should either provide all models with equivalent input information or report additional metrics under consistent conditions to demonstrate that the counterfactual approach itself provides benefits beyond having more input context.

comments.title: Questionable 'unsupervised' claim due to training data requirements
comments.quote: Our method does not require the knowledge or supervision of any ground truth positions or other object or scene properties.
comments.explanation: This claim appears misleading given the paper's actual methodology. The abstract states the method requires no supervision of ground truth positions or properties, but the paper-level context reveals that training requires paired counterfactual trajectories (AB, CD). Having access to counterfactual outcomes provides significant implicit supervision about physical parameters - the model learns confounders by observing how outcomes change under interventions. This differs from truly unsupervised physics learning from single trajectories. The abstract should be revised to clarify that supervision comes through counterfactual pairs rather than direct labels. Rewrite as 'Our method does not require explicit labels for positions or physical properties, learning instead from paired counterfactual trajectories.'

comments.title: Identifiability definition has logical flaw
comments.quote:
###### Definition 1

(Identifiability, *(Pearl, 2018)*) The experiment $(\mathbf{AB},\mathbf{CD},z)$ is identifiable if, for any set of confounders $z^{\prime}$:

$\psi(\mathbf{A},z)=\psi(\mathbf{A},z^{\prime})\Rightarrow\psi(\mathbf{C},z)=\psi(\mathbf{C},z^{\prime}).$ (1)
comments.explanation: The identifiability definition is logically incorrect. It states that if two confounder sets produce the same observed trajectory AB, they must also produce the same counterfactual trajectory CD. However, this condition is vacuously true when the premise is false - if no two different confounder sets produce the same AB trajectory, the experiment would be considered 'identifiable' even though confounders might not be uniquely determined from AB. The correct definition should require that confounders are uniquely determined by the observed trajectory: $\psi(\mathbf{A},z)=\psi(\mathbf{A},z^{\prime}) \Leftrightarrow z=z^{\prime}$. This ensures that AB uniquely identifies the confounders, which is what the text claims is needed for well-posedness. Rewrite Definition 1 as: 'The experiment $(\mathbf{AB},\mathbf{CD},z)$ is identifiable if, for any set of confounders $z^{\prime}$: $\psi(\mathbf{A},z)=\psi(\mathbf{A},z^{\prime}) \Rightarrow z=z^{\prime}$' because this properly captures the requirement that confounders can be uniquely retrieved from the observed trajectory.

comments.title: Index error in coefficient notation
comments.explanation: The text states that each keypoint k has a coefficient vector \mathbf{c}_k of size C, but equation (3) uses \mathbf{c}_k^{C+1} which would be out of bounds. This creates an inconsistency between the definition and usage. The term \mathbf{c}_k^{C+1} appears to be an indexing error, likely intended to be \mathbf{c}_k^0 or some other valid index within 1..C. Rewrite the equation as \mathbf{G}_k^i = \mathbf{c}_k^0 \mathbf{c}_k^i (\mathcal{G}(\mathbf{k}_k) * \mathbf{H}_i) or clarify that \mathbf{c}_k has size C+1.

comments.title: Inconsistent L-PSNR values in Table 3
comments.explanation: The L-PSNR values for B-CF (27.88, 27.16, 27.07, 26.33, 27.07, 26.73) are higher than the corresponding PSNR values (21.19, 21.33, 21.37, 20.49, 21.09, 20.97). This is mathematically impossible since L-PSNR is computed on masked regions focusing on moving objects, which should be more challenging to predict than the full scene including static background. The PSNR metric typically achieves higher values than localized variants because background reconstruction is easier. These values appear to be swapped or contain a calculation error. Rewrite the B-CF row with corrected L-PSNR values that are lower than the PSNR values.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated training instability claim
comments.quote: End-to-end training of all three modules jointly is challenging, as the same pipeline controls both the keypoint-based state representation and the dynamic module (CoDy), involving two adversarial objectives: optimizing reconstruction pushes the keypoint encoding to be as representative as possible, but learning the dynamics favors a simple representation. Faced with these two contradictory tasks, the model is numerically unstable and rapidly converges to regression to the mean.
comments.explanation: The claim about numerical instability and convergence to regression to the mean lacks supporting evidence. The text states that reconstruction favors 'representative' encoding while dynamics favors 'simple' representation, but neither term is formally defined. No empirical results comparing joint versus separate training are provided, nor is there any mathematical analysis of the optimization landscape. This assertion should either be removed or supported with concrete evidence such as training curves, loss comparisons, or theoretical analysis of the conflicting objectives. Rewrite the passage to either remove the unsubstantiated claims or add supporting evidence.

comments.title: Assumption of no camera motion not justified
comments.quote: Exploiting an assumption on the absence of camera motion, the goal is to favor the emergence of disentangling static and dynamic information.
comments.explanation: The paper states it exploits the absence of camera motion but provides no justification for why this assumption holds in the datasets or how violations are handled. Since camera motion would invalidate the disentanglement objective (static features would not remain static), this needs clarification. Add: 'We verify that all sequences in dataset \mathbf{D} were captured with stationary cameras; for sequences with camera motion, the model would require additional motion compensation modules' after this sentence.

comments.title: Ambiguous training data source for CoDy
comments.quote: Then we freeze the parameters of the keypoint detector and train CoDy to forecast the keypoints from  $\mathbf{D}$  minimizing the loss in Equation (6).
comments.explanation: The description of training CoDy to 'forecast the keypoints from \mathbf{D}' is ambiguous. If \mathbf{D} represents counterfactual outcomes as suggested in the counterfactual framework, then training directly on \mathbf{D} would not learn causal dynamics. The text should clarify whether CoDy is trained to predict \mathbf{D} given interventions \mathbf{C} (learning causal dynamics) or whether it's simply trained to reproduce \mathbf{D} sequences. Clarify this by specifying the exact training objective: 'train CoDy to predict counterfactual keypoints \mathbf{D} given observed sequence \mathbf{AB} and intervention \mathbf{C}' or 'train CoDy to reproduce keypoints in \mathbf{D}'.

comments.title: Undefined gradient operator in loss
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{deren}}=\gamma_{1}\left|\left|\mathbf{X}_{\text{target}}-\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{\text{target}}\right|\right|_{2}^{2}+\gamma_{2}\left|\left|\nabla\mathbf{X}_{\text{target}}-\nabla\hat{\mathbf{X}}_{\text{target}}\right|\right|_{2}^{2},$ (4)
comments.explanation:
The loss function uses the gradient operator 
abla on images \mathbf{X}_{	ext{target}} and \hat{\mathbf{X}}_{	ext{target}}, but the paper does not define whether 
abla computes spatial gradients (horizontal/vertical) or includes temporal gradients. Since the model processes image pairs from video sequences, this ambiguity affects implementation. Add clarification: 'where 
abla denotes the spatial gradient operator computing partial derivatives with respect to image coordinates' after equation (4).

comments.title: Undefined notation in equation (5)
comments.quote: $\mathcal{GN}(\mathbf{x}_{k})=\mathbf{\hat{x}}_{k},\text{ such that }\mathbf{\hat{x}}_{k}=g\left(\mathbf{x}_{k},\sum_{i}\mathbf{e}_{ik}\right)\text{ with }\mathbf{e}_{ij}=f\left(\mathbf{x}_{i},\mathbf{x}_{j}\right),$ (5)
comments.explanation: The equation defines \mathbf{e}_{ij} but uses \mathbf{e}_{ik} in the summation. This creates a notational inconsistency where the edge function is defined with indices (i,j) but used with (i,k). Rewrite equation (5) as: \mathcal{GN}(\mathbf{x}_{k})=\mathbf{\hat{x}}_{k},\text{ such that }\mathbf{\hat{x}}_{k}=g\left(\mathbf{x}_{k},\sum_{i}\mathbf{e}_{ik}\right)\text{ with }\mathbf{e}_{ik}=f\left(\mathbf{x}_{i},\mathbf{x}_{k}\right) to maintain consistent indexing.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for state vectors
comments.quote: We define the state of frame $\mathbf{X}_{t}$ at time $t$ as a stacked vector composed of keypoints and coefficients computed by the encoder (the de-rendering module), i.e. $\mathbf{s}(t)=[\mathbf{s}_{1}(t)\text{ ... }\mathbf{s}_{k}(t)]$ where $\mathbf{s}_{k}(t)=[\mathbf{k}_{k}\text{ \ }\mathbf{c}_{k}^{1}\text{... }\mathbf{c}_{k}^{C+1}](t)$.
comments.explanation: The text defines the state as \mathbf{s}(t) with K components \mathbf{s}_{k}(t), but the notation uses lowercase k in the subscript \mathbf{s}_{k} while the summation range uses uppercase K. Later in the same paragraph, the text refers to \mathbf{h}_{K}(t) with uppercase K, creating inconsistency. Rewrite as: \mathbf{s}(t)=[\mathbf{s}_{1}(t)\text{ ... }\mathbf{s}_{K}(t)] where \mathbf{s}_{k}(t)=[\mathbf{k}_{k}\text{  }\mathbf{c}_{k}^{1}\text{... }\mathbf{c}_{k}^{C+1}](t) for k=1..K to maintain consistent notation.

comments.title: Missing dimension specification for confounder vectors
comments.quote: Its last hidden vector is taken as the confounder estimate $\mathbf{u}_{k}$.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces \mathbf{u}_{k} as the confounder representation but never specifies its dimensionality. This is crucial for understanding how the confounders are concatenated with state embeddings later in the text: 'It takes the projected state embeddings \sigma_{k}^{\mathbf{CD}}(t) per keypoint k concatenated with the confounder representation \mathbf{u}_{k}'. Add 'of dimension d_u' after this sentence: 'Its last hidden vector is taken as the confounder estimate \mathbf{u}_{k} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_u}'.

comments.title: Counterfactuality definition notation inconsistency
comments.quote:
Definition 2 (Counterfactuality). Let  $z^k$  be the set of confounders  $z$ , where the  $k^{th}$  value has been modified. The experiment (AB, CD,  $z$ ) is counterfactual if and only if:

$\exists k: \psi (\mathbf {C}, z ^ {k}) \neq \psi (\mathbf {C}, z).$ (2)
comments.explanation: The definition introduces $z^k$ as 'the set of confounders $z$, where the $k^{th}$ value has been modified' but then uses $z^k$ in the existential quantifier without defining what modification means. The notation is ambiguous - does $z^k$ represent any possible modification to the $k^{th}$ confounder, or a specific modification? The definition should clarify that $z^k$ represents $z$ with the $k^{th}$ confounder changed to some different value. Rewrite as: 'Let $z^k$ denote the confounder set $z$ with the $k^{th}$ confounder modified to a different value. The experiment (AB, CD, $z$) is counterfactual if and only if: $\exists k, \exists v \neq z_k: \psi(\mathbf{C}, z^k(v)) \neq \psi(\mathbf{C}, z)$' because this removes the ambiguity about what constitutes a modification.

comments.title: Table 4 missing baseline comparison symbol
comments.quote: |  State auto-encoder: | ✓ | ✗  |
comments.explanation: Table 4 shows 'State auto-encoder: | ✓ | ✗' but the text describes comparing 'projection to 256 dimensions to the baseline reasoning directly in keypoint + coefficient space'. The table header should clarify what ✓ and ✗ represent. Based on the text, ✓ likely indicates the CoDy encoder (high-dimensional space) and ✗ indicates the baseline (keypoint + coefficient space). Add explicit labels to the table header to make the comparison clear.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a method for unsupervised counterfactual physical reasoning in pixel space, utilizing a hybrid latent representation of dense features, 2D keypoints, and shape coefficients. The authors introduce a new benchmark, Filtered-CoPhy, and demonstrate that their approach outperforms existing video prediction baselines on counterfactual tasks.

The paper provides a well-motivated approach to the difficult problem of unsupervised counterfactual reasoning in high-dimensional pixel space. The hybrid latent representation and the use of Koopman-inspired dynamics modeling are conceptually sound and show strong empirical performance. The work is a significant step forward in moving beyond 3D-supervised benchmarks.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in Confounder Identification: The paper claims that confounders (masses, velocities) are extracted from the observed trajectory AB. However, Section 4.2 describes this as an unsupervised estimation process using a GRU over keypoint sequences. It is unclear how the model distinguishes between intrinsic physical parameters and transient state variables without explicit structural constraints. If the model simply learns a mapping from motion to future states, it may be encoding trajectory patterns rather than physical properties. The authors should provide an ablation or analysis showing that the learned confounder representation is invariant to the specific initial conditions of the observed experiment.

- Potential for Shortcut Learning in Pixel Space: The model relies on a de-rendering/rendering pipeline where static background information is separated via a dense feature map F. While this is intended to isolate dynamics, the reconstruction loss in Equation 4 might encourage the model to 'cheat' by encoding dynamic object information into the static background features if the keypoint representation is insufficient. The paper lacks a rigorous test to demonstrate that the model is truly learning physical dynamics rather than just predicting pixel-wise motion patterns. Adding a stress test with out-of-distribution physical parameters (e.g., varying gravity or friction significantly beyond the training range) would clarify if the model has learned the underlying physics or merely interpolated training data.

- Sensitivity of the Hybrid Representation: Section 4.1 introduces a hybrid representation combining keypoints with shape/appearance coefficients. The decoder uses a fixed filter bank H to deform Gaussian heatmaps based on these coefficients. There is no proof or empirical sensitivity analysis showing that this specific architecture is not overly constrained by the choice of H. If the object shapes in the test set deviate significantly from the 'elliptical' bias imposed by the filter bank, the model's performance likely degrades. The authors should demonstrate the model's robustness by testing on objects with non-convex geometries or significantly different aspect ratios.

- Identifiability and Well-Posedness: Definition 1 in Section 4 defines identifiability, but the paper does not provide a formal proof or a diagnostic check that the datasets used actually satisfy this condition. Since the confounders are unobserved, it is theoretically possible that multiple sets of physical parameters produce indistinguishable trajectories in the pixel space. Without a more rigorous discussion of the mapping from the latent space to the physical parameter space, it is hard to guarantee that the counterfactual predictions are physically grounded. The authors should include an analysis of the 'identifiability gap' for the specific scenarios (Blocktower, Balls, Collision) to show that the latent confounders are unique.

- Lack of quantitative evidence for causal intervention accuracy: The paper claims to perform counterfactual reasoning by predicting the effect of interventions (displacing, re-orienting, or removing objects). However, the results in Table 1 and Table 2 focus on reconstruction error (PSNR) and keypoint-space MSE. These metrics evaluate predictive accuracy but do not distinguish between the model's ability to simulate physical dynamics versus its ability to perform causal interventions. To validate the causal claims, the authors should report the 'Intervention Accuracy' or 'Counterfactual Error' specifically for the subset of test samples where an object is removed or displaced, comparing the model's prediction against the ground-truth simulation of the same intervention.

- Missing demonstration of physical parameter generalization: The paper argues that the model learns physical dynamics rather than just pixel-wise motion. To demonstrate that the model has actually captured physical laws like mass-dependent collisions, the paper should include a simulation where physical parameters (e.g., the mass of a ball in the BallsCF scenario) are varied outside the range seen during training. Showing that the model's counterfactual predictions remain physically consistent—such as correctly predicting the change in collision trajectories when mass ratios are shifted—would provide the necessary evidence that the latent confounders are capturing physical properties rather than just interpolating training trajectories.

- Absence of a sensitivity analysis for the fixed filter bank: The model uses a fixed filter bank H to deform Gaussian heatmaps into object shapes. While the authors argue this is a design choice, the paper does not show how sensitive the model is to the geometry of the objects being modeled. To ensure the representation is not overly constrained, the authors should provide a qualitative and quantitative analysis of the model's performance when the objects have non-convex or highly irregular geometries that cannot be easily represented by the current filter bank. This would confirm whether the 'hybrid representation' is truly general or limited to the specific elliptical-like shapes used in the benchmark.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper presents a compelling and well-executed method, but it requires further validation regarding the physical grounding of the learned latent variables and the robustness of the hybrid representation. Addressing the potential for shortcut learning and providing a more rigorous check on the identifiability of confounders would significantly strengthen the contribution.

Revision targets:

- Perform an out-of-distribution evaluation where physical parameters like mass or gravity are sampled outside the training distribution to test for genuine physical reasoning.

- Provide a diagnostic analysis of the learned confounder space to demonstrate that it captures physical properties rather than just trajectory-specific motion features.

- Include a sensitivity study on the fixed filter bank H to determine how much the model's performance depends on the assumption of elliptical object shapes.

- Clarify the identifiability of the confounders by empirically demonstrating that the model can recover similar latent parameters for different trajectories generated by the same physical parameters.

comments.title: Logical insufficiency of the identifiability definition
comments.quote:
(Identifiability, *(Pearl, 2018)*) The experiment $(\mathbf{AB},\mathbf{CD},z)$ is identifiable if, for any set of confounders $z^{\prime}$:

$\psi(\mathbf{A},z)=\psi(\mathbf{A},z^{\prime})\Rightarrow\psi(\mathbf{C},z)=\psi(\mathbf{C},z^{\prime}).$ (1)
comments.explanation: The condition in Equation (1) only ensures that the counterfactual outcome is independent of the confounder value given the same observed trajectory. It does not guarantee that the confounder $z$ is uniquely retrievable from the observation $\mathbf{AB}$. If multiple distinct confounders produce the same trajectory, the model cannot uniquely identify the physical parameters. Please revise the definition to require that $\psi(\mathbf{A},z)=\psi(\mathbf{A},z^{\prime}) \Rightarrow z=z^{\prime}$ for the experiment to be identifiable.

comments.title: Contradiction in state decoder definition
comments.quote: The state encoder $\mathcal{E}(\mathbf{s}(t))=\bm{\sigma}(t)$ is modeled as a graph network $\mathcal{GN}$, whose aggregation function projects into an output embedding space $\bm{\sigma}(t)$ of dimension 256. The decoder $\Delta(\bm{\sigma}(t))=\mathbf{s}(t)$ temporally processes the individual contextualized states $\bm{\sigma}(t)$ with a GRU
comments.explanation: The notation $\Delta(\bm{\sigma}(t))=\mathbf{s}(t)$ implies a frame-wise mapping, but the description of the decoder involves a GRU, which is a temporal operator. If $\Delta$ is a temporal operator, it cannot map a single state to a single state without historical context. Furthermore, the loss in Equation 6 implies an identity mapping on individual frames, which contradicts the GRU-based temporal processing. Please clarify whether $\Delta$ operates on a window of states and reconcile this with the loss function.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of CoPhy supervision
